steps.n: Connected the root letters together with kasra on first letter and sukun on middle letter to form the basic noun
Adding 'al' makes it definite and doubles the ت with a shadda
Adding kasra at the end shows this word is connected to something after it
Adding wa at the beginning connects this word to what comes after it. The alif changes to a special form (ٱ) after wa
The root is extended to form the word for 'olive'
Connected the root letters together to form the basic noun for 'oil'
Added ون to change oil into olive - a common pattern for plant names
Added ال to make it specific
Added kasra at the end because it comes after a preposition
Added و at beginning to connect it to the previous word وَٱلتِّينِ (and the fig)
Added the vowel mark 'oo' sound (damma) to make it mean a specific mountain
Added kasra at the end to show this mountain belongs to or is connected to سِينِينَ (Sinai)
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it
This is the base form of the proper noun referring to Mount Sinai or the region around it. It doesn't have a root as it's a borrowed proper name.
Added ين to make it plural, which can refer to the multiple peaks or regions of Sinai

steps.m: Sinai (plural form, in genitive case after وَطُورِ)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end because this word follows وَطُورِ (and Mount) in the ayah, showing it's being described
Added the alif with a bar (ٰ) and fatha marks to show correct pronunciation
The و (and) is added to connect this word with what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic noun

steps.m: the city (described by الْأَمِينِ - the secure/trusted)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific ('the city') and kasra at the end because it's being described by the word الْأَمِينِ (the secure/trusted) that comes after it
The root letters convey the basic idea of safety and trust
Adding fatḥa, kasra and yā creates an adjective meaning 'having the quality of being trustworthy'
Adding ال makes it specific. This word describes البلد (the city) in this verse
The kasra ending shows this describes the word البلد (city) that comes before it
Added sukun at the end to show this is a complete particle. قَد is used to emphasize that something definitely happened or to show it already occurred.
Added لَ at the beginning for extra emphasis. This combination لَقَدْ strongly emphasizes the verb that follows it (خَلَقْنَا meaning 'We created').
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Added sukun on last letter to prepare for the suffix
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action. This word is connected to الْإِنسٰنَ (mankind) which is what was created.

steps.m: to be social, friendly with others
steps.n: Forms the word إِنسٰن (human) from the concept of being sociable
Root meaning 'to be social' became a noun for 'mankind' by changing vowels - kasra at start makes it a noun
Adding ان to إِنْس creates the singular word for 'human being'
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific

steps.m: the human being (object of خَلَقْنَا - we created)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is what was created. The alif became long (ٰ) for writing style.
Basic preposition showing location or position. In this ayah, it connects to the word أَحْسَنِ (best) to show 'in the best'.
Added an elongation mark (ٓ) which shows the sound is stretched when reading, but doesn't change the meaning
Forms the superlative 'best, most excellent'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic adjective
Added أَ at the beginning and sukun on first root letter to show comparison
Added kasra at the end to show this describes the word تَقْوِيمٍ (stature/form) that comes after it

steps.m: to stand straight, to rise up
steps.n: Connected the root letters together to form the base word
Added تَ at start and ي after و to change from 'standing straight' to 'making something straight' - like taking something crooked and fixing it
Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) to show this word is owned by or connected to 'best' (أَحْسَنِ), like saying 'in best form'
This is a connecting word (particle) that shows one thing happens after another. It connects this ayah to what came before it.

steps.m: then (connects to رَدَدْنٰهُ - We returned him)
steps.n: Added the vowel marks ُ (damma) and َ (fatha) with shadda to show how it's pronounced. The shadda doubles the م sound.
Added vowels to connect the letters and make a past tense verb
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action
Added هُ at the end to show who was returned. The alif in نَا changes to ٰ when connected to هُ.

steps.m: to be low or at the bottom

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: lowest (describing سٰفِلِينَ - those who are low)
steps.n: Added أَ at the beginning and changed the middle vowel to sukun to make it mean 'lowest'. This word is describing سٰفِلِينَ (those who are low) in this ayah.
Added alif after first letter and kasra after second letter to create the active participle form, showing someone/something that has this quality
Added ين to make the word plural, showing multiple people/things that are low
Added fatha at the end to show the word is in the accusative case

This is the basic form of the exception particle that introduces what is excluded from a previous statement. In this ayah, it introduces those who are excluded from loss - the believers who do good deeds.

Added shadda and alif to strengthen and complete the exception meaning. This particle connects to 'those who' (ٱلَّذِينَ) that follows it, excluding the believers from the previous statement about loss.
This is a relative pronoun used to describe a group of people
Changed the first letter to hamza and added alif, which makes the meaning stronger and more specific to religious faith

steps.m: they believed (connected to وَعَمِلُوا۟ - and they did)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show many people did this action. This word connects to وَعَمِلُوا۟ (and they did) in the ayah, showing these are the same group of people.
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb
Changed from singular 'he' to plural 'they' by adding وا at the end
Added و at beginning to connect this action with the previous word ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed), showing these people both believed and did good works
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who is righteous/good (acted on by عَمِلُوا)
steps.n: Changes from 'he was good' to 'good/righteous person' by adding a long ا and changing the middle vowel. Shows who has this quality, like changing 'succeed' to 'successful'.
Changes from singular 'righteous' to feminine plural 'righteous deeds' by adding ات at the end
Makes it specific with ٱل and shows its grammatical role with the kasra ending. The shadda emphasizes the ص because it comes after ٱل.
Added fatha to the basic preposition ل to connect it with other parts
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement about those who believe and do good deeds

steps.m: so for them (connected to أَجْرٌ - reward)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show this is 'for them' - referring back to those who believe and do good deeds. This connects to the word أَجْرٌ (reward) that comes after it.
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to make it a noun meaning the result of rewarding
Added double dammah to make it indefinite (a reward)
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make a basic noun. The ي with sukun (ْ) makes a long 'ay' sound.
Added damma (ُ) at the end to show this word is in the nominative case, like a subject of a sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Changes verb (he gave) into noun meaning 'thing that is given'
Added tanween kasra to show this word is describing the reward (أَجْرٌ) mentioned in the ayah
This is the basic interrogative particle that asks questions like 'what'
Added فَ at the beginning to show this question follows from something before. This word is asking what could make you deny religion after seeing these signs.
This is the basic form of lying
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger and more intense
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added كَ at the end to show who is being denied (you)
The root meaning of distance developed into a word meaning 'after' in time or sequence
Connected the letters and added vowel marks. The idea of distance in space became distance in time, leading to the meaning 'after'.
Added damma at the end to show this word is the main subject being discussed. In this ayah, it refers to what comes after the previous statements.

steps.m: to follow a path or way
steps.n: The meaning evolved from 'debt/obligation' to 'judgment/religion'
Added kasra (ِ) under first letter to make it a noun meaning the system or way you follow
Added ال (the) at the beginning which makes the د double (with ّ)
Added kasra (ِ) at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the word before it (بِ)
Added بِ at beginning to mean 'with/by'. This word is connected to يُكَذِّبُكَ (denies you) in the ayah - it shows what is being denied.
This is a special verb used for negation
Adding vowel marks to make it a usable verb meaning 'is not'
Added أَ at the beginning to turn this into a question. This is asking about Allah (اللَّهُ) in this ayah.
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the basic word for 'deity'

This is the unique name for the One True God in Islam. The ال at the beginning and the shadda on ل make it specific and emphasize its uniqueness. The ُ at the end shows this is the subject of the sentence.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Forms the elative pattern أفعل which indicates 'more/most' of the root meaning
The -i ending shows this word is connected to something after it, like saying 'the most wise OF something'

steps.m: by/with the most wise (connected to الْحٰكِمِينَ)
steps.n: The word is connected to الْحٰكِمِينَ (the judges/wise ones) in this ayah, asking if Allah isn't the wisest of all judges
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the word to show who does the action
Made the word plural to show many judges
Added 'the' to point to specific judges

steps.m: the wisest of judges (object of بِأَحْكَمِ)
steps.n: Added a final mark to show this word is being acted upon. In this ayah, it's connected to بِأَحْكَمِ (most wise) to ask 'Isn't Allah the wisest of judges?'
